name: Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools
districtFact: North Carolina's fourth largest school district, WSFCS is recognized for one of the state's most comprehensive Career and Technical Education (CTE) systems — with high school pathways in healthcare, IT, engineering technology, and construction trades that directly feed Winston-Salem's medical, manufacturing, and tech employer base. The district's Early College partnership with Forsyth Technical Community College allows qualifying students to earn a full associate degree alongside their high school diploma.

about.title: About Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools
about.content: WSFCS serves approximately 55,000 students across 80+ schools in Winston-Salem and Forsyth County. For the 2026–2027 school year, all schools operate on a traditional calendar — no year-round schools are in operation within the district. As North Carolina's fourth largest school system, WSFCS is notable for its depth of Career and Technical Education: high school programs in healthcare, advanced manufacturing, IT, and construction trades. These pathways reflect Winston-Salem's own economy — Atrium Health Wake Forest Baptist is the city's largest employer, and advanced manufacturing remains a significant regional industry. College-bound students have access to an Early College program at Forsyth Technical Community College, enabling dual-enrollment students to earn an associate degree alongside their diploma. Pre-K programs are available for income-qualifying families and children with identified special needs.

planningTips: WSFCS starts August 10 — roughly two weeks before Charlotte-Mecklenburg (Aug 25). If your household has children in both districts, expect a two-week window where WSFCS students are already in school but CMS children are not — and a similar gap when spring break arrives.

WSFCS Spring Break (Mar 26–Apr 2) and Guilford County's break (Mar 22–26) share only one overlap day: Good Friday, March 26. Forsyth families with Guilford relatives can coordinate just that one day without pulling kids from school.

Wake Forest University is located in Winston-Salem but is entirely separate from WSFCS. WFU's semester schedule — including fall reading days, finals weeks, and graduation — has no connection to WSFCS school dates or closures.

In the 2026–2027 school year, WSFCS ends May 21 — among the earliest last days in North Carolina. WSFCS families can begin summer programs and childcare nearly three weeks before Wake County (June 9, 2027) and Charlotte-Mecklenburg (June 9, 2027) complete their school year.

districtFaqs.q: Is Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools the same as Wake Forest University?
districtFaqs.a: No. Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools (WSFCS) is the K-12 public school system serving Winston-Salem and Forsyth County. Wake Forest University is a separate private research university that happens to be located in Winston-Salem. Despite the shared 'Wake' name, the two institutions are completely unrelated. Wake Forest University's semester schedule has no connection to WSFCS school dates. Wake Forest University relocated from the town of Wake Forest (in Wake County) to Winston-Salem in 1956, which is why the name can cause confusion.

For the 2026–2027 school year, all Winston-Salem/Forsyth County Schools operate on a traditional school calendar with a summer break. There are no year-round or modified-calendar schools currently operating within the district.

Yes. WSFCS operates Pre-K programs for income-qualifying families and for children with identified developmental needs or disabilities. Enrollment is not guaranteed for all four-year-olds — contact WSFCS directly for current eligibility requirements and seat availability.

districtFaqs.q: How are snow days or emergency closings made up in WSFCS?
districtFaqs.a: North Carolina requires public school districts to meet the state's minimum instructional time requirements. WSFCS builds weather makeup days into its calendar as a buffer. If those buffer days are exhausted, the district may extend the school year into the summer. The actual impact on the last day of school depends on how many emergency days are used in a given year.

districtFaqs.q: Does WSFCS have magnet or specialty academic programs?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. WSFCS offers Career and Technical Education (CTE) academies at the high school level and an Early College program in partnership with Forsyth Technical Community College. The Early College program allows qualifying high school students to take college courses and potentially earn an associate degree alongside their diploma. Contact individual high schools for program requirements and application deadlines.

districtFaqs.q: Do all WSFCS schools follow the same school calendar?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. WSFCS operates on a single traditional school calendar applied to all schools in the district. There are no separate year-round tracks or modified-calendar schools — all WSFCS students share the same first day, last day, and holiday schedule.

livingHere.intro: Winston-Salem is a mid-sized Piedmont city in the midst of a decades-long economic transition — from its roots as the tobacco and textile capital of the South toward a growing identity anchored by one of the largest medical school complexes in the Southeast and a vibrant arts scene. Families relocating here find lower housing costs than Charlotte or Raleigh, a walkable arts district, and a city with a strong sense of its own distinct identity.

highlights.label: Atrium Health Wake Forest Baptist
highlights.detail: Wake Forest Baptist Medical Center (now part of Atrium Health) is Winston-Salem's largest employer, housing a level-one trauma center and one of the Southeast's leading academic medical research programs. It is a primary driver of professional relocations into WSFCS boundaries — particularly for healthcare workers and research staff.

highlights.label: Wake Forest University
highlights.detail: Wake Forest University, a private research university with roughly 9,000 students, is located in Winston-Salem's northern suburbs. Its campus events, athletics, and community partnerships shape local life throughout the school year, but the university's academic calendar is entirely separate from WSFCS school dates.

highlights.label: Kaleideum — Children's Science and Discovery
highlights.detail: Kaleideum (formerly the Children's Museum of Winston-Salem and the SciWorks science center) is the city's primary family cultural destination. With hands-on science exhibits and interactive programming, it is a popular outing for WSFCS families on teacher workdays, school breaks, and holiday weekends.

highlights.label: Forsyth County Public Library
highlights.detail: The Forsyth County library system operates multiple branches across Winston-Salem and the county suburbs. Summer reading programs, homework help, and after-school activities make the library system a consistent resource for WSFCS families outside of school hours.

highlights.label: Suburban Family Zones – Clemmons, Lewisville, Kernersville
highlights.detail: Most WSFCS families with school-age children cluster in Forsyth County's western suburbs (Clemmons, Lewisville) or the eastern side (Kernersville). These communities offer newer housing stock and lower property costs than comparable Charlotte suburbs, while remaining fully within WSFCS school boundaries.

highlights.label: Arts District and SECCA
highlights.detail: The Arts District along Trade Street and the Southeastern Center for Contemporary Art (SECCA) make Winston-Salem one of the more arts-active mid-size cities in the South. The North Carolina School of the Arts (NCSA), a nationally ranked conservatory for high school and college students, is also located here.

relatedDistricts.name: Guilford County Schools
relatedDistricts.reason: Neighboring Piedmont Triad district. Families comparing or moving between Forsyth and Guilford counties commonly search both calendars — spring break overlaps only on Good Friday, leaving just one shared day without pulling kids from school.

relatedDistricts.name: Wake County Public School System
relatedDistricts.reason: North Carolina's largest district and a frequent comparison for families relocating within the state. WSFCS ends nearly three weeks before Wake County — parents moving between Forsyth and Wake counties often check both calendars to time enrollment and summer care.

relatedDistricts.name: Charlotte-Mecklenburg Schools
relatedDistricts.reason: Largest district in the Piedmont region. Parents moving between Charlotte and Winston-Salem, or managing childcare across both districts, frequently compare the two calendars — WSFCS starts two weeks earlier than CMS and ends three weeks earlier.

sources.label: North Carolina Department of Public Instruction
